questions.question: Define electric current and potential difference. Explain the relationship between them.
questions.answer: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge (I = Q/t). Potential difference is the work done per unit charge in moving charge from one point to another (V = W/Q). They are related by Ohm's law: V = IR, where current is directly proportional to potential difference at constant temperature.

questions.question: State Ohm's law and explain it with the help of a V-I graph.
questions.answer: Ohm's law states that current through a conductor is directly proportional to potential difference across it at constant temperature (V = IR). The V-I graph is a straight line through origin; slope gives resistance R.

questions.question: Describe an experiment to verify Ohm's law.
questions.answer: Set up circuit with battery, rheostat, ammeter, voltmeter, and resistor. Vary potential difference using rheostat, record V and I values. Plot V vs I graph; straight line confirms Ohm's law. Slope = R.

questions.question: Explain the factors on which resistance of a conductor depends.
questions.answer: Resistance R depends on: length l (R ∝ l), area of cross-section A (R ∝ 1/A), material (resistivity ρ), temperature (increases with temperature for metals). Formula: R = ρl/A.

questions.question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance when three resistors are connected in series.
questions.answer: In series, same current I flows through each. Total V = V1 + V2 + V3 = IR1 + IR2 + IR3 = I(R1 + R2 + R3). Thus, Req = R1 + R2 + R3.

questions.question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance when three resistors are connected in parallel.
questions.answer: In parallel, same V across each. Total I = I1 + I2 + I3 = V/R1 + V/R2 + V/R3 = V(1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3). Thus, 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3.

questions.question: Explain the advantages of parallel combination over series combination in domestic wiring.
questions.answer: In parallel, voltage same across devices, each operates independently. If one fails, others continue. In series, current same; one failure breaks circuit. Parallel allows different power devices.

questions.question: Explain Joule's law of heating and its applications.
questions.answer: Heat produced H = I²Rt (Joule's law). Applications: electric heater (nichrome coil), bulb filament (tungsten), fuse (low melting point wire melts on overload), toaster.

questions.question: Define electric power. Derive expressions for power in terms of V, I, R.
questions.answer: Electric power is rate of electrical energy consumption (P = VI). From V = IR, P = I²R or P = V²/R.

questions.question: Explain commercial unit of electrical energy and how to calculate electricity bill.
questions.answer: Commercial unit is kilowatt-hour (kWh) = 1 unit = 3.6 × 10^6 J. Energy = Power × time (kWh). Bill = units × rate per unit.

questions.question: Explain why nichrome is used in heating appliances.
questions.answer: Nichrome has high resistivity and high melting point, produces more heat (H = I²Rt) without melting easily. Oxidation resistant, durable for heaters/toasters.

questions.question: Explain the role of fuse in domestic circuits.
questions.answer: Fuse is safety device with low melting point wire. On overload/short circuit, high current melts wire, breaks circuit, prevents fire/accident. Placed in live wire.

questions.question: Describe the factors affecting resistivity of a material.
questions.answer: Resistivity ρ depends on material nature and temperature. Increases with temperature for metals (free electrons collide more), decreases for semiconductors. Independent of dimensions.

questions.question: Explain why transmission of electric power is done at high voltage.
questions.answer: Power loss = I²R. High voltage reduces current for same power (P = VI). Lower current means lower I²R loss in transmission lines, efficient long-distance power transfer.

questions.question: Explain the difference between ohmic and non-ohmic conductors with examples.
questions.answer: Ohmic: obey Ohm's law, V-I straight line (e.g., metal wire at constant temp). Non-ohmic: do not obey, V-I curve (e.g., filament bulb - resistance increases with temp; diode - unidirectional).

questions.question: Calculate the equivalent resistance when resistors are connected in mixed series-parallel combination.
questions.answer: First find parallel equivalent (1/Rp = 1/R + 1/R), then add series resistors. Example: two 4Ω parallel (Rp=2Ω) in series with 6Ω → Req = 2 + 6 = 8Ω.

questions.question: Explain earthing in domestic electrical circuits.
questions.answer: Earthing connects metal body of appliances to ground via earth wire. Provides low-resistance path for leakage current, prevents shock. Earth wire green/yellow, connected to ground pit.

questions.question: Explain the heating effect of electric current with formula and one numerical application.
questions.answer: Heating effect: H = I²Rt. Example: 5A current through 10Ω resistor for 2 min → H = 25 × 10 × 120 = 30000 J = 30 kJ.

questions.question: Explain why metals like copper are used for electrical transmission wires.
questions.answer: Copper has low resistivity, good conductor, low power loss (I²R small), ductile for wires, corrosion resistant, cost-effective for long-distance transmission.

questions.question: Describe domestic electric circuits with diagram.
questions.answer: Live wire (red), neutral (black), earth (green). Parallel connections for appliances, fuse/MCB in live wire, earthing for safety. Main switch and distribution board.

questions.question: Explain the principle behind electric fuse and MCB.
questions.answer: Fuse: low melting wire melts on overload. MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker): electromagnet trips switch on overload. Both protect from short circuit/fire; MCB resettable.

questions.question: Calculate power dissipated in a resistor and explain energy conservation.
questions.answer: Power P = I²R or V²/R. Energy conserved: electrical energy converts to heat/light. Example: 100W bulb at 220V → I = P/V = 0.45A, R = V²/P = 484Ω.

questions.question: Explain temperature dependence of resistance in conductors and semiconductors.
questions.answer: Conductors: resistance increases with temperature (more collisions). Semiconductors: decreases (more charge carriers released). Thermistors use this property.

questions.question: Discuss the importance of earthing in preventing electric shocks.
questions.answer: Earthing provides low-resistance path to ground for fault current, trips circuit breaker/fuse, prevents user shock. Metal appliances earthed via green wire.

questions.question: Explain the working of an electric bulb and why tungsten is used as filament.
questions.answer: Current heats tungsten filament to incandescence, emits light. Tungsten: high melting point (3422°C), high resistivity, doesn't oxidise easily in vacuum/inert gas.
